bull_thesis: Aptiv completed a credible, on-time transformation from a diversified conglomerate into a focused pure-play: the low-margin, copper-indexed, peso-exposed Electrical Distribution Systems business was spun off as Versigent (VGNT) on April 1, 2026, leaving a higher-quality 'New Aptiv' consisting of Intelligent Systems (~$5.8B revenue) and Engineered Components (~$6.7B revenue). The investment case rests on four evidence-backed pillars. First, genuine and improving free cash flow: $818M operating cash flow in the fourth quarter of 2025, trailing twelve months (the prior four quarters combined) free cash flow of $1.09B, a three-year free cash flow compound annual growth rate of +54%, and a price-to-free-cash-flow ratio of 9.8x — funding both debt paydown and buybacks, including a $3B accelerated share repurchase (a pre-arranged buyback executed all at once) that retired roughly 20% of shares outstanding over twelve months. Second, best-in-class execution: a 100% earnings-per-share beat rate across seven consecutive quarters, a Piotroski score (a nine-point balance sheet health test) of 8 out of 9, gross margin of 19.1% and expanding, selling and administrative costs cut 10% then a further 5%, and a first-quarter 2026 result of $1.71 versus consensus of $1.62 confirming the cadence. Third, improving revenue mix: roughly 25% of revenue now comes from outside automotive — aerospace and defense, telecom, and data centers are approaching $4 billion combined — with Wind River (an edge-software subsidiary) growing above 20% against a $6B addressable market, plus early-stage robotics and drones optionality via partnerships with Robust AI, Vecna Robotics, and Comau. Fourth, bookings momentum: $7B of new contract awards in the first quarter of 2026 (+15% versus the 2025 average), a $20B-plus New Aptiv 2026 bookings target, and signs of China local-market share gains and export-platform traction. If the market re-rates a deleveraging, software-tilted New Aptiv toward a quality-compounder multiple of 12–14x from the post-spin price near $54, there is upside — conditional on converting bookings into 4–7% revenue growth, holding margins on a currency-adjusted basis, continuing to pay down the $6.2B net debt, and proving the robotics investment is shifting the earnings mix rather than simply weighing on it.
bear_thesis: The central structural catalyst — the Electrical Distribution Systems spin-off — has already fired (completed April 1, 2026), the re-rating it was meant to drive is spent, and the stock actually fell roughly 10% on the first-quarter 2026 earnings print, signaling that the 'New Aptiv' growth premium is being priced down, not up. What remains is a cyclical Tier-1 auto supplier with difficult underlying economics: a 2025 GAAP net margin of 0.8%, GAAP earnings per share of $0.75 (down 89% year over year), a five-year earnings-per-share compound annual growth rate of -21%, return on invested capital of 6.8% (below the cost of capital), $6.18B in net debt, and profit margins running 1.65 standard deviations below their historical peak. The 'record adjusted earnings per share' reported every quarter is the product of financial engineering — an 18–20% reduction in share count combined with exclusions for amortization, restructuring, foreign exchange, and other items — not genuine earnings growth. Insider conviction is negative: two open-market stock purchases (9,800 shares) versus fifteen sales (113,488 shares). Demand is structurally pressured: chronic below-market growth in China (New Aptiv China revenue was -14% in the first quarter of 2026), program cancellations by Zeekr and NIO that earlier optimism had not flagged, an ongoing roll-off of legacy infotainment content, electric-vehicle content-mix headwinds, and a segment rename — from 'Advanced Safety and User Experience' to 'Intelligent Systems' — that repackaged a declining legacy business as a growth platform. Bookings credibility has a visible crack: the $31B 2025 target was missed at $27B, blamed on 'timing' in two consecutive years. The Mexican peso is a structural, partially unhedgeable operating-income drag. On peer valuations the stock is expensive, not cheap: Lear, Magna, and BorgWarner all trade at 6–8x price-to-free-cash-flow with better cash conversion and higher returns on invested capital; Aptiv's optically low 9.8x price-to-free-cash-flow flatters near-zero net margins (trailing price-to-earnings is roughly 79x). Robotics is embryonic and explicitly described as an 'incremental investment continuing into 2026' — a margin headwind before it becomes a margin driver. The bear case is confirmed if revenue growth stalls below 4%, robotics remains a cost center, China remains in negative territory, and the market continues to price Aptiv as the cyclical supplier it still is.
consensus_delta: Where we disagree with the market: the usual question for a deep-value signal is whether the street is pricing terminal decline into a company with stable or growing earnings power. Here the answer is no — the street is actually too optimistic, not too pessimistic. The 16-analyst consensus carries a 'Buy' rating with an average price target near $92 (versus a spot price near $54.50), implicitly pricing in a full 'New Aptiv' software and robotics re-rating that the already-completed spin no longer catalyzes and that the GAAP economics (0.8% net margin, -89% earnings per share, 6.8% return on invested capital) do not support. The factually incorrect street assumption is that post-spin multiple expansion is still ahead of us; in reality the catalyst is already behind us, price targets are being cut (Citi reduced its target from $92 to $77 on May 19), and the stock dropped roughly 10% on the first-quarter 2026 print. The screener's discounted-cash-flow fair value of $56 and analyst targets sit at the optimistic end of the range; the earnings-power-value model and Buffett-style intrinsic-value estimate (both pointing to $21.80, implying a margin of safety of -85%) and the peer comparisons (expensive versus Lear and Magna) are the corrective anchors. The genuine market disagreement here is the mirror image of a typical deep-value opportunity: consensus is looking the wrong way by being too bullish on a spent-catalyst cyclical, not too bearish on a hidden compounder.
forcing_function: The catalyst that was supposed to force a re-rating — the Electrical Distribution Systems spin-off — has already occurred: Versigent (VGNT) was distributed to shareholders on April 1, 2026. There is no remaining hard-dated corporate event that compels the market to reprice the stock. The next scheduled milestones are ordinary earnings releases (second-quarter 2026 results expected around July 2026, roughly 47 days from the scan date) at which management must demonstrate that China revenue turned positive and that margins expanded on a currency-adjusted basis — but those are 'show-me' checkpoints, not binding catalysts, and a miss looks more probable than a beat given the foreign-exchange, China, and auto-production backdrop. Our investment framework favors hard-dated catalysts inside a favorable window and penalizes past or out-of-window ones and spent re-ratings — Aptiv is the textbook penalize case: a heavily covered mega-cap whose single transformational event is now two months in the rear-view mirror, the opposite of the lightly covered, structurally mis-priced special situations (distressed deleveragings, spinoffs with forced sellers) where the highest-conviction opportunities arise.
valley_of_death: The risky window — the period over the next three to nine months before any re-rating could materialize — is populated almost entirely by downside risks. First, auto-production deterioration: management conceded 'a deterioration in underlying vehicle production' in the first quarter of 2026, with electric-vehicle growth assumptions running 5–6 percentage points below IHS forecasts; a hard landing in North American or European auto output would compress volumes directly. Second, the China drag persists: New Aptiv's China revenue was -14% in the first quarter of 2026; management's promise that it will turn 'positive in Q2' is a forecast, not a fact, and the risk of further program cancellations similar to Zeekr and NIO is live. Third, the foreign-exchange and peso operating-income drag continues — Versigent absorbed the unhedged operational exposure but New Aptiv still faces foreign-exchange and commodity headwinds, with the peso having moved from roughly 21 to below 18. Fourth, the robotics investment is an explicit, growing margin headwind described by management as 'continuing into 2026' — it consumes cash before it generates returns. Fifth, $6.2B in net debt against 0.8% net margins leaves almost no room for error; rising input costs (resins, metals, copper) and customer recovery lags pressure near-term free cash flow. Sixth, the broader macro backdrop (late-cycle tape, oil near $92, inflation re-accelerated to 3.8%, the Federal Reserve on hold) squarely exposes a cyclical auto supplier to the dominant market risk with no offsetting characteristics.
positioning_washout: Forced-selling pressure is limited — and most of what existed has already played out. Aptiv is a heavily covered (16 analysts) large-cap with a broad institutional shareholder base (institutional flow score 60 out of 100, smart-money score 33 out of 100); it is not a yield or passive-income name whose holders are forced to liquidate on a dividend cut. The one genuine mechanical forced-selling event — the April 1 Versigent distribution — already compelled index-rebalancing and mandate-driven selling (holders received Versigent shares and some funds unable to hold the spun-off company sold it, repricing the parent). That flush is behind us. The roughly 10% stock drop on the first-quarter 2026 print and the post-spin price-target cuts represent the repricing process, not a one-time panic that establishes a clean entry point. There is no identifiable forced-seller catalyst ahead that would create an asymmetric buying opportunity; the shareholder base is sticky, long-only institutional money, not a crowded trade set up for a sharp pain-driven reversal.
moderator_conclusion: Pass for now. This is a competently run, financially sound business — Piotroski score 8 out of 9, seven consecutive earnings beats, genuinely improving free cash flow — that just completed a credible structural pivot. But the investment committee question is timing and asymmetry, and on both counts Aptiv falls short. The load-bearing catalyst, the Electrical Distribution Systems spin into Versigent, fired on April 1, 2026; the re-rating that catalyst was supposed to deliver is spent; the stock already fell roughly 10% on the first-quarter 2026 earnings print; and analysts are cutting price targets (Citi from $92 to $77). Our framework rewards hard-dated catalysts inside a favorable window and penalizes those that are past or out-of-window — Aptiv is the textbook penalize case, a heavily covered large-cap whose single transformational event is two months in the rear-view mirror, the opposite of the lightly covered, structurally mispriced special situations where high-conviction opportunities arise. On valuation, a sum-of-the-parts analysis (valuing each of the two remaining business lines separately) of the post-spin New Aptiv — covering only Intelligent Systems and Engineered Components, since the Electrical Distribution Systems segment has been distributed and is stale in the screener data — reconciles to roughly $32 per share in the base case (bull case ~$44, bear/earnings-power-value floor ~$21), while the stock trades near $54.50, roughly 70% above that intrinsic estimate. Peer comparisons confirm the stock is expensive rather than cheap: Lear and Magna trade at 6–8x price-to-free-cash-flow with better returns on invested capital. The deep-value signal here is misclassified: the street is too bullish (the consensus $92 price target prices in a re-rating the spent catalyst can no longer deliver), not too bearish. The near-term risky window — auto-production deterioration, China revenue down 14%, foreign-exchange headwinds, robotics investment burn, $6.2B of debt against 0.8% net margins — skews the next three to nine months to the downside. Verdict: pass for now, conviction 2. On a purely valuation basis, independent of the catalyst and timing picture, the conclusion is the same: the business is operationally high quality but trades meaningfully above its parts and expensively versus peers, with below-cost-of-capital returns on invested capital and negative open-market insider conviction. Both lenses — the catalyst lens (fired) and the value lens (above fair value) — independently say pass.
sop_breakdown: The base case reconciles the bull (~$44) and bear (~$21) midpoints for the post-spin two-segment New Aptiv only, excluding the distributed Electrical Distribution Systems business, which is now a separately traded instrument (Versigent). Intelligent Systems: roughly $5.8B revenue at approximately 1.2x enterprise-value-to-sales (enterprise value divided by revenue) equals roughly $7.0B enterprise value. Engineered Components: roughly $6.7B revenue at approximately 0.85x enterprise-value-to-sales equals roughly $5.7B enterprise value. Combined segment enterprise value is roughly $12.7B. Subtract New Aptiv net debt of roughly $5.5B to arrive at equity value of roughly $7.2B; add a modest robotics and edge-AI optionality premium of roughly $0.3B, giving total equity of roughly $7.5B. Divided across roughly 233 million shares, the base-case sum-of-the-parts estimate is roughly $32 per share. This is anchored against the methodology models — the discounted-cash-flow fair value of $56 is the optimistic bound, the earnings-power-value and Buffett intrinsic-value estimate of $21.80 is the pessimistic bound — and disciplined by peer comparisons to Lear, Magna, and BorgWarner. The post-spin market price of roughly $54.50 as of May 15 sits above this base-case estimate; the stock is not cheap on a parts basis.
risk_reward: Unfavorable. The post-spin price of roughly $54.50 versus a base-case sum-of-the-parts fair value of roughly $32 means the stock trades approximately 70% above its estimated intrinsic parts. Downside to fair value is roughly -41% (to $32); downside to the bear-case earnings-power-value floor is roughly -61% (to $21). Upside to the bull-case sum-of-the-parts estimate (~$44) is negative from the current price. The street's average price target of $92 implies roughly $22 of 're-rating' upside, but that upside depends on multiple expansion that the already-completed spin can no longer catalyze. Risk-to-reward is approximately 1.5 to 1 against — roughly -41% to fair value versus a speculative upside the underlying parts do not support.
catalyst_status: FIRED — The load-bearing catalyst was the EDS spin-off. Verified via SEC 8-K and press (Yahoo Finance, May 2026): Versigent (NYSE: VGNT) began regular-way trading April 1, 2026; record date March 17, 2026; distribution 1 VGNT per 3 APTV. The distribution is COMPLETE and PAID — re-rate is spent. The stock fell ~10% on the Q1-26 print (2026-05-05), and analysts have been CUTTING PTs post-spin (Citi to $77 from $92, May 19, 2026). What remains ('New Aptiv' margin/robotics re-rate) is a SOFT, un-dated, single-binary multiple-expansion story, not an asymmetric special-situation.
peer_comps_note: Peer comparison verdict: expensive versus peers. The true peer group consists of Lear (LEA), Magna (MGA), BorgWarner (BWA), Visteon (VC), Forvia, Continental, and Delphi — all Tier-1 automotive content suppliers competing in wiring harnesses and high-voltage architecture. Lear trades at roughly 6–7x price-to-free-cash-flow with better cash conversion; Magna trades at roughly 7–8x with higher returns on invested capital. Aptiv's optically low 9.8x price-to-free-cash-flow flatters near-zero net margins (0.8%) and earnings per share that fell 89% in 2025 (trailing price-to-earnings is roughly 79x). The valuation premium versus Lear and BorgWarner is not justified by the high-voltage architecture investment thesis given the execution risks. The enterprise-value-to-sales multiples used in the sum-of-the-parts analysis — roughly 1.2x for Intelligent Systems and roughly 0.85x for Engineered Components — are deliberately anchored to the peer band; richer model outputs such as the discounted-cash-flow fair value of $56 are not supported by where comparable companies trade. Conclusion: Aptiv is fair-to-expensive, not a value name, on a relative basis.
interrogator_dossier:
# Aptiv PLC (APTV) — Forensic Dossier

Transcripts reviewed: Q1 2025 (May 1, 2025) through Q1 2026 (May 5, 2026), six consecutive quarters spanning the run-up to and completion of the Electrical Distribution Systems (EDS) spin-off into Versigent (VGNT, NYSE), distributed April 1, 2026.

---

## 1. Narrative Arc

The story over these six quarters is a deliberate, well-telegraphed transformation from a diversified conglomerate into a focused pure-play, executed against a deteriorating auto-production backdrop. It moved through three phases.

Phase 1 — 'Resilient operator weathering the storm' (Q1–Q2 2025). The opening narrative was defensive and macro-dominated: tariffs, USMCA compliance, China electric-vehicle customer mix, and the Mexican peso. CEO Kevin Clark led every earnings call with the 'in-region, for-region' supply-chain digital twin as the company's competitive advantage. Revenue was flat to down ('Revenue totaled $4.8 billion, down 1%,' Q1-25), and the entire pitch was operating execution offsetting external headwinds — selling, general and administrative costs down 10% in 2024 with another 5% targeted for 2025, 'more aggressive in terms of looking for opportunities to further reduce costs' (Q1-25). The EDS separation was described as 'on track' but remained background noise.

Phase 2 — 'The spin becomes the story' (Q3 2025–Q4 2025). As 2025 progressed, the separation moved to center stage. By the fourth-quarter 2025 call (February 2, 2026) management had named the entity ('Versagen,' with CEO Joe Liatine), set the date ('spin out... on April 1'), and quantified the stranded-cost drag ($70M flagged at Investor Day; '$50 million impact in the first full year on a pro-forma basis' for New Aptiv plus '~$15 million' public-company setup for the spun-off entity). Simultaneously, the segment architecture was relabeled: 'Advanced Safety and User Experience' became 'Intelligent Systems' in Q4-25, reframing the legacy advanced-driver-assistance-systems (ADAS) and infotainment business as a software-and-edge-compute growth engine.

Phase 3 — 'New Aptiv: intelligence at the edge' (Q1 2026). After the separation completed April 1, 2026, Clark's prepared remarks were 'focused exclusively on New Aptiv.' The narrative pivoted sharply from auto-supplier-managing-decline to diversified software and edge-compute platform: 'sense, think, act and continually optimize,' roughly 25% non-automotive revenue, Wind River (a $6B addressable market), and a new robotics and drones growth vector via partnerships with Robust AI, Vecna Robotics, and Comau. The center of gravity shifted from cost reduction and tariff management to addressable-market expansion and bookings ($7B total awards in Q1-26; $20B-plus New Aptiv 2026 bookings target).

Growth drivers introduced: robotics, collaborative robots, autonomous mobile robots, and drones (new in Q4-25 and Q1-26); Wind River edge-AI ecosystem (steadily amplified, 'over 20%' growth); non-auto end markets including aerospace and defense, telecom, data centers, and industrial (described as 'rapidly approaching almost $4 billion' by Q3-25). Drivers quietly de-emphasized: the tariff and USMCA crisis framing that dominated Q1–Q2 2025 faded once it proved 'de minimis'; the China local-OEM mix headwind that was flagged for four straight quarters was reframed in Q1-26 as on track to turn into 'positive growth in Q2.'

---

## 2. Claims Versus Financials

The central tension: management claimed record results every single quarter — record revenue, operating income, and earnings per share — while the actual full-year financials show a company whose net income collapsed from $2.94B (2023) to $165M (2025) and earnings per share from $10.39 to $0.75. The reconciliation is that management speaks almost exclusively in adjusted, ex-amortization, ex-restructuring, ex-special-items, and ex-foreign-exchange terms — and the gap between that adjusted scorecard and GAAP reality is the central forensic story.

Management claim: 'record... earnings per share of $2.17' / '$1.86' / 'record EPS' (Q3-25, Q4-25). Hard financials: full-year 2025 GAAP earnings per share of $0.75, down 89.2% year over year; five-year earnings-per-share compound annual growth rate of -21.1%. Assessment: diverges sharply. The adjusted 'records' are driven heavily by an 18–20% share-count reduction from the $3B accelerated share repurchase completed April 1, 2025 — a smaller denominator, not a larger numerator.

Management claim: 'strong operating performance... flow-through on volume' (every quarter). Hard financials: full-year net margin 0.8%; return on invested capital 6.8% (below the cost of capital); gross margin 19.1% (expanding). Assessment: partial. The gross-margin expansion and the cost-reduction program are real and corroborated. But near-zero net margins and below-cost-of-capital returns say operating strength is being consumed below the gross-profit line by amortization, interest on $6.2B in net debt, and restructuring charges.

Management claim: '$818M / $584M / $510M operating cash flow... accelerate deleveraging' (Q2–Q4-25). Hard financials: trailing twelve months free cash flow of $1.09B (down 5.4% year over year but three-year compound annual growth rate of +54%); price-to-free-cash-flow 9.8x; net debt $6.18B. Assessment: largely supported. Cash generation is genuine and the deleveraging claim is credible. Free cash flow is the real asset here, not accounting earnings per share.

Management claim: '$31 billion in new business awards this year' (Q1–Q3-25, reaffirmed). Hard financials: delivered $27B (Q4-25: 'short of our target of $31 billion'). Assessment: missed. A clean, dated guidance miss — the first hard credibility dent. Management blamed timing ('customer awards shifting to 2026'), which is plausible but is exactly the deflection analysts flagged all year.

Management claim: 'confident in reaching... over $31 billion' — target subsequently lowered without fanfare. New Aptiv 2026 bookings guide: '$20 billion' (not comparable post-spin because the business perimeter changed). Assessment: reset, not missed. The bar moved with the perimeter; reasonable, but the serial 'timing shift' language is now a pattern.

Management claim: margin 'expansion of 30 basis points, excluding FX and commodities' (Q1-26). Hard financials: margins running 1.65 standard deviations below their historical peak; net margin 0.8%. Assessment: the 'excluding foreign exchange' qualifier is load-bearing. On a reported basis margins compressed — Q4-25 Intelligent Systems margins were 'surprisingly weak' with a -170 basis point foreign-exchange hit. The ex-FX framing is honest as a like-for-like measure but flatters a business taking real foreign-exchange losses.

Net read: the cash story — free cash flow, deleveraging — matches the numbers. The earnings story is rosier than the underlying trajectory justifies because it leans on adjustments and a shrinking share count to manufacture 'records' atop an 89% GAAP earnings-per-share collapse.

---

## 3. Tone and Confidence Trajectory

Management's confidence is remarkably consistent — almost scripted — but the content shifted from offense to defense and back.

Q1-25: Cautious and defensive. 'We're currently in a period of uncertainty due to rapid changes in global trade policies'; management explicitly declined to guide for the second half ('we don't have the visibility to forecast revenue for Q3 and Q4'). Confident on cost control, evasive on demand.

Q2-25: Steady. 'Record second quarter results,' but management proactively flagged 'ongoing roll-off of legacy user experience programs' and a 'slowdown... on select Zeekr and NIO programs in China... headwinds for the next few quarters' — naming future weakness before it fully arrived, which is a positive honesty signal.

Q3-25: Most offensively confident of the year. '$8.4 billion of new business awards,' full-year guidance raised — but immediately hedged on trade policy ('we just overlaid some incremental conservatism').

Q4-25: Mixed. Confident on the spin and bookings, but management had to acknowledge the $31B bookings miss and explain 'surprisingly weak' Intelligent Systems margins (analyst Colin Langan's words, conceded by Clark). Defensive on foreign exchange and the peso.

Q1-26: Re-energized and offensively framed. Full 'intelligence at the edge' investment-thesis language, robotics addressable-market claims, '$20 billion of bookings... high level of confidence.' The tone reset to growth and investment language precisely as the perimeter narrowed to the higher-margin remaining company.

Specificity over time: management grew more specific on bookings and segment detail but persistently less specific on forward revenue, repeatedly declining to provide full-year or second-half guidance in 2025. The shift from offensive themes (addressable market, robotics, edge AI) to defensive themes (cost cuts, tariff mitigation, foreign-exchange hedges) and back maps cleanly to the macro cycle and the spin timeline.

---

## 4. Guidance Credibility

Reliability is medium-to-high on the controllables, medium on externals.

Earnings beat rate: 7 out of 7 (100%), corroborated by the Q1-26 print ($1.71 versus $1.62 consensus). This is a genuine, durable pattern of setting a beatable bar and clearing it, aided by the buyback-driven share-count reduction.

The one hard miss: the $31B bookings target fell short at $27B (Q4-25). Management reaffirmed '$31 billion' in Q1, Q2, and Q3 before conceding the shortfall. The miss was roughly 13%, attributed entirely to timing. Credibility cost is real but limited because bookings are a softer metric than earnings per share, and the 'shifted to 2026' explanation is at least internally consistent (the 2026 guide is $20B-plus for the smaller New Aptiv).

Cost and cash guidance: reliably delivered. The selling and administrative cost reduction program (-10% in 2024, -5% in 2025) and the deleveraging and buyback cadence were stated and executed: the accelerated share repurchase completed on schedule, retiring 20% of shares over twelve months.

Pattern: sandbag earnings per share and deliver; over-promise bookings and under-deliver once; execute cost and capital-allocation plans precisely. Net: high credibility on earnings per share and capital allocation, medium on commercial momentum (bookings).

---

## 5. Analyst Pressure Points

China mix and below-market growth (every quarter, the dominant recurring pressure). Wells Fargo analyst Colin Langan pressed this in Q1, Q2, and again on Versigent's electric-vehicle exposure in Q4. Management's answers were consistently directional and non-quantified — 'I'm not sure we'll give you an exact dollar amount on the headwind' (Q3-25). The Zeekr and NIO program cancellations named in Q2-25 became the bad news that earlier 'on track to match production mix' optimism had soft-pedaled. This is the clearest pattern of deflecting and then delivering bad news.

Second-half visibility (2025). Langan in Q1-25 walked management into the implied second-half acceleration ('a pretty major step up in growth over market') and Clark simply declined: 'we're not providing guidance for the second half.' Analysts wanted a forecast; management offered a framework.

In-house versus outsourced advanced driver assistance systems (Q2-25, Tom Narayan/RBC). A strategic-risk probe — do large vehicle manufacturers pull ADAS development in-house? Management answered confidently ('that trend has reversed course'), a claim about competitor behavior that warrants ongoing monitoring.

Versigent stranded costs and pro-forma earnings bridge (Q4-25, James Picariello). Sharp, technical pressure on the $75M earnings gap, stranded costs ($70M), and company-setup costs — management answered cleanly, a positive signal.

Intelligent Systems margin weakness (Q4-25, Langan). 'Surprisingly weak' was the analyst's characterization — management conceded three drivers: a -170 basis point foreign-exchange hit, engineering-credit timing, and the robotics investment. Honest, but it confirmed the segment's margins are lumpy and foreign-exchange-sensitive.

What analysts worry about that management is not fully addressing: whether the bookings-to-revenue conversion is real given timing slips in two consecutive years, and whether non-automotive and robotics is a genuine margin mix-shifter or an investment sink ('incremental investment... will continue into 2026,' Q4-25).

---

## 6. Concerns and Positives

Concerns:

GAAP earnings collapse masked by 'record' adjusted earnings per share. Full-year 2025 GAAP earnings per share of $0.75 (-89%) versus a steady drumbeat of 'record' adjusted quarters — the gap is bridged by adjustments plus an 18–20% share-count reduction. The 'records' are partly financial engineering.

Serial bookings timing slips. The $31B-to-$27B miss, blamed on slippage to 2026, followed by a 2026 guide that itself acknowledges awards 'could shift.' A two-year pattern of moving targets forward.

Insider conviction is negative. Open-market reality: 2 purchases (9,800 shares) versus 15 sales (113,488 shares) — the grant-inflated buy ratio in summary data is misleading; the real signal is net selling. No insider is meaningfully buying into the 'New Aptiv' re-rating story.

China revenue chronically below market growth, with program cancellations (Zeekr and NIO) that earlier optimism had not flagged; New Aptiv China was down 14% in Q1-26.

The Mexican peso is a structural, recurring operating-income drag that management cannot fully hedge (peso from roughly 21 to below 18; described as causing 'a ton of operating-income impact'), and Versigent carries the unhedged operational exposure.

Positives:

Free-cash-flow generation is genuine and improving: $818M operating cash flow in Q4-25; trailing twelve months free cash flow of $1.09B; three-year compound annual growth rate of +54% — funding both buybacks and debt paydown, and representing the real asset of this business.

The spin was executed on time and as promised — Versigent/VGNT distributed April 1, 2026 — meaning management did exactly what it said over four quarters, a credibility deposit.

Proactive disclosure of bad news: Zeekr and NIO roll-offs and legacy-program decline were named before they fully hit earnings, and technical stranded-cost questions were answered cleanly.

100% earnings-per-share beat rate across 7 quarters, and a Piotroski balance-sheet health score of 8 out of 9 — operationally the business is well-run and financially sound despite the headwinds.

Real cost discipline: selling and administrative costs cut 10% then 5%; seven facilities consolidated — the controllables are managed tightly.

---

## 7. Hidden Signals

The segment rename is informative. 'Advanced Safety and User Experience' became 'Intelligent Systems' in Q4-25. This is not purely cosmetic; it reframes a business carrying a declining legacy infotainment and user-experience content roll-off as a forward-looking edge-compute platform, conveniently timed as the spin approached and the equity story needed a growth label.

Two different names for the spun-off company appear in the transcripts — 'Versagen' in Q4-25 and Q1-26 prepared remarks and the SEC and market name 'Versigent' (ticker VGNT) — with both terms used loosely in the Q1-26 transcript. The 'Versigent' closing-day reference matches the filed entity. This is cosmetic but worth noting: the transcript transcription is imperfect.

Capital-allocation language shifted from buybacks to a broader toolkit. Early 2025 was dominated by the completed $3B accelerated share repurchase. By Q1-26 the framing is 'allocated both organically and inorganically... while also returning capital' — meaning acquisitions are back on the table for New Aptiv. Watch for a bolt-on acquisition in software or robotics, which would represent a pivot in the use of cash.

Joe Liatine (Versigent CEO) appears in Q4-25; his presence signals the spun-off company is a genuine operating entity, not a financial shell. The robotics partnerships — Robust AI, Vecna Robotics, Comau — are new commercial relationships introduced only in the last two quarters, an embryonic and unproven optionality layer being presented as a growth pillar.

The competitive framing around in-sourcing risk softened ('OEMs... less desire to do things internally,' Q2-25) precisely when that risk was most topical. This is a confident assertion that, if incorrect, undercuts the entire ADAS investment case.

---

## 8. Capital-Allocation Verdict

Classification: transformation structurally complete, moving to stable post-spin.

interrogator_dossier:
# Aptiv PLC (APTV) — Interrogator Forensic Dossier

*Transcripts: Q1 2025 (2025-05-01) through Q1 2026 (2026-05-05), six consecutive quarters spanning the run-up to and completion of the Electrical Distribution Systems (EDS) spin-off into Versigent (VGNT, NYSE), distributed April 1, 2026.*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story over these six quarters is a deliberate, well-telegraphed **conglomerate-to-pure-play transformation** executed against a deteriorating auto-production backdrop. It moved through three phases:

**Phase 1 — "Resilient operator weathering the storm" (Q1–Q2 2025).** The opening narrative was defensive and macro-dominated: tariffs, USMCA compliance, China EV-customer mix, FX (the Mexican peso). Kevin Clark led every call with the "in-region, for-region" supply-chain digital twin as the moat. Revenue was flat-to-down ("Revenue totaled $4.8 billion, down 1%," Q1-25), and the entire pitch was *operating execution offsetting external headwinds* — SG&A down 10% in 2024, targeted another 5% in 2025, "more aggressive in terms of looking for opportunities to further reduce costs" (Q1-25). The EDS separation was mentioned as "on track" but was background.

**Phase 2 — "The spin becomes the story" (Q3 2025–Q4 2025).** As 2025 progressed, the separation moved to center stage. By Q4-25 (2026-02-02) management had named the entity ("Versagen," CEO Joe Liatine), set the date ("spin out... on April 1"), and quantified the stranded-cost drag ($70M called out at Investor Day, "$50 million impact in the first full year on a pro forma basis" for NewAptiv plus "~$15 million" public-company setup for the SpinCo). Simultaneously the segment architecture was relabeled: "Advanced Safety and User Experience" became **"Intelligent Systems"** (Q4-25), reframing the legacy ADAS/infotainment business as a software-and-edge-compute growth engine.

**Phase 3 — "New Aptiv: intelligence at the edge" (Q1 2026).** Post-separation (completed April 1, 2026), Clark's prepared remarks were "focused exclusively on New Aptiv." The narrative pivoted hard from *auto-supplier-managing-decline* to *diversified software/edge-compute platform*: "sense, think, act and continually optimize," ~25% non-automotive revenue, Wind River ($6B TAM), and a brand-new **robotics/drones** growth vector (partnerships with Robust AI, Vecna Robotics, Comau). The center of gravity shifted from cost-out and tariffs to TAM expansion and bookings ($7B total awards Q1-26; $20B+ NewAptiv 2026 bookings target).

**Growth drivers introduced:** robotics/cobots/AMRs and drones (new in Q4-25/Q1-26); Wind River edge-AI ecosystem (steadily amplified, "over 20%" growth); non-auto end markets (A&D, telecom, data centers, industrial — "rapidly approaching almost $4 billion" by Q3-25). **Drivers quietly de-emphasized:** the original tariff/USMCA crisis framing (Q1-Q2 2025's dominant topic) faded once it proved "de minimis"; the China-local-OEM-mix-headwind, hammered for four straight quarters, was reframed in Q1-26 as turning "positive growth... in Q2."

---

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

The headline tension: management claimed **"record" results every single quarter** (record Q-revenue, OI, EPS) while the actual fiscal-year financials show a company whose net income collapsed from $2.94B (2023) to $165M (2025) and EPS from $10.39 to $0.75. The reconciliation is that management speaks almost exclusively in **adjusted, ex-amortization, ex-restructuring, ex-special-items, ex-FX/commodity** terms — and the gap between that scorecard and GAAP reality is the central forensic story.

| Management claim | Quarter | Hard financials | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "record... earnings per share of $2.17" / "$1.86" / "record EPS" | Q3-25, Q4-25 | FY2025 GAAP EPS **$0.75**, down **-89.2% YoY**; 5-yr EPS CAGR **-21.1%** | **Diverges sharply.** Adjusted "records" are driven heavily by an 18–20% share-count reduction (the $3B ASR completed Apr-1-2025), not earnings growth. The denominator, not the numerator, makes the records. |
| "strong operating performance... flow-through on volume" | every Q | FY net margin **0.8%**; ROIC **6.8%** (below WACC); Gross margin 19.1% (expanding) | **Partial.** Gross-margin expansion and the SG&A program are real and corroborated. But net margin near zero and sub-cost-of-capital ROIC say operating strength is being consumed below the line (amortization, interest on $6.2B net debt, restructuring). |
| "$818M / $584M / $510M operating cash flow... accelerate deleveraging" | Q2–Q4-25 | TTM FCF **$1.09B** (down -5.4% YoY but 3-yr CAGR +54%); P/FCF 9.8x; Net debt **$6.18B** | **Largely supported.** Cash generation is genuine and the deleveraging claim is credible. This is the strongest pillar — FCF is the real asset here, not accounting EPS. |
| "$31 billion in new business awards this year" | Q1–Q3-25 (reaffirmed) | Delivered **$27B** (Q4-25: "short of our target of $31 billion") | **Missed.** A clean, dated guidance miss — the first hard credibility dent. Management blamed timing ("customer awards shifting to 2026"), which is plausible but is exactly the deflection analysts flagged all year. |
| "confident in reaching... over $31 billion" → silently lowered | Q1-26 | NewAptiv 2026 bookings guide **"$20 billion"** (not comparable post-spin) | **Reset, not missed.** The bar moved with the perimeter; reasonable, but the serial "timing shift" language is now a pattern. |
| Margin "expansion of 30 basis points, excluding FX and commodities" | Q1-26 | Margin **-1.65σ vs historical peak**; Net margin 0.8% | **The ex-FX qualifier is load-bearing.** On a reported basis margins compressed (Q4-25 Intelligent Systems margins were "surprisingly weak," -170bps FX hit). The "ex-FX/commodity" framing is honest as a same-store metric but flatters a business taking real FX losses. |

**Net read:** the cash story (FCF, deleveraging) **matches** the numbers; the earnings story is **richer than the trajectory justifies** because it leans on adjustments and a shrinking share count to manufacture "records" atop a -89% GAAP EPS collapse.

---

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: STABLE-to-mildly-defensive, with a confident pivot in the final quarter.** Management's confidence is remarkably consistent — almost scripted — but the *content* of that confidence shifted from offense to defense and back:

- **Q1-25:** Cautious-defensive. "we're currently in a period of uncertainty due to rapid changes in global trade policies"; explicitly **declined to guide** H2 ("we don't have the visibility to forecast revenue for Q3 and Q4"). Confident on cost control, evasive on demand.
- **Q2-25:** Steady. "record second quarter results," but flagged "ongoing roll-off of legacy user experience programs" and "slowdown... on select Zeekr and NIO programs in China... headwinds for the next few quarters" — *proactively naming* future weakness (a green-flag honesty signal).
- **Q3-25:** Most confident of the year offensively. "$8.4 billion of new business awards," raised full-year guidance — but immediately hedged on Nexperia and trade ("we just overlaid some incremental conservatism").
- **Q4-25:** Mixed. Confident on the spin and bookings, but had to own the $31B miss and explain "surprisingly weak" Intelligent Systems margins (Colin Langan's words, conceded by Clark). Defensive on FX/peso.
- **Q1-26:** Re-energized, offensive. Full "intelligence at the edge" investment-thesis framing, robotics TAM, "$20 billion of bookings... high level of confidence." The tone reset to growth/investment language precisely as the perimeter narrowed to the higher-margin RemainCo.

**Specificity over time:** Management got *more* specific on bookings and segment detail but persistently *less* specific (deliberately) on forward revenue, repeatedly refusing full-year/half-year guidance in 2025 ("we're not providing guidance for the second half"). The shift from "offensive" (TAM, robotics, edge AI) to "defensive" (SG&A -5%, tariff mitigation, FX hedges) and back maps cleanly to the macro cycle and the spin timeline.

---

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: MEDIUM-HIGH on the controllables, MEDIUM on the externals.**

- **Earnings-beat rate: 7/7 (100%)** per the screener, corroborated by the Q1-26 print ($1.71 vs $1.62 consensus). This is a genuine, durable sandbagging-and-deliver pattern on EPS — management consistently sets a beatable bar and clears it, helped by the buyback tailwind.
- **The one hard miss:** the **$31B bookings target → $27B actual** (Q4-25). They reaffirmed "$31 billion" in Q1, Q2, and Q3 ("we continue to expect roughly $31 billion") before conceding the shortfall. The miss was ~13%, blamed entirely on timing. Credibility cost: real but limited, because bookings are a softer KPI than EPS and the "shifted to 2026" explanation is at least internally consistent (2026 guide is $20B+ for the smaller NewAptiv).
- **Cost/cash guidance: reliably delivered.** The SG&A reduction program (-10% 2024, -5% 2025) and the deleveraging/buyback cadence were stated and executed (ASR completed on schedule, 20% of shares retired over 12 months).
- **Pattern:** Sandbag EPS, deliver; over-promise bookings, under-deliver once; execute cost/capital plans precisely. Net: **High** on EPS and capital allocation, **Medium** on commercial momentum (bookings).

---

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **China mix / growth-over-market (every quarter, the dominant recurring pressure).** Colin Langan (Wells Fargo) pressed it in Q1, Q2, and again on Versagen EV exposure in Q4. Management's answers were consistently directional and non-quantified — "I'm not sure we'll give you an exact dollar amount on the headwind" (Q3-25). The Zeekr/NIO program cancellations (named Q2-25) became the bad news that the earlier "we're on track to match production mix" optimism had soft-pedaled. **This is the clearest deflect-then-deliver-bad-news pattern.**
- **Second-half visibility (2025).** Langan in Q1-25 walked management into the implied H2 acceleration ("a pretty major step up in growth over market") and Clark simply refused: "we're not providing guidance for the second half." Analysts wanted a forecast; management gave a framework.
- **In-source vs outsource ADAS (Q2-25, Tom Narayan/RBC).** A strategic-risk probe — do large OEMs pull ADAS in-house? Management answered confidently ("that trend has reversed course"), which is a *claim about competitors' behavior* that bears watching.
- **Versagen stranded costs and pro-forma EBITDA bridge (Q4-25, James Picariello).** Sharp, technical pressure on the $75M EBITDA gap, stranded costs ($70M), and standup costs — management answered cleanly, a green flag.
- **Intelligent Systems margin weakness (Q4-25, Langan).** "surprisingly weak" — management conceded three drivers (FX -170bps, engineering-credit timing, robotics investment). Honest, but it confirmed the segment's margins are lumpy and FX-exposed.

**What analysts worry about that management isn't fully addressing:** whether the bookings-to-revenue conversion is real (timing slips two years running), and whether non-auto/robotics is a genuine margin mix-shifter or an investment sink ("incremental investment... will continue into 2026," Q4-25).

---

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **GAAP earnings collapse masked by "record" adjusted EPS.** FY2025 GAAP EPS $0.75 (-89%) vs a steady drumbeat of "record" adjusted quarters — the gap is bridged by adjustments + an 18–20% share-count reduction. The "records" are partly financial engineering.
🔴 **Serial bookings "timing shifts."** The $31B → $27B miss, blamed on slippage to 2026, then a 2026 guide that itself notes awards "could shift." A two-year pattern of moving the goalposts forward.
🔴 **Insider conviction is negative.** Open-market reality: **2 buys (9,800 sh) vs 15 sells (113,488 sh)** — the grant-inflated buy-ratio in the scan is misleading; the real signal is net selling. No insider is backing up the truck on the "New Aptiv" re-rate story.
🔴 **China growth-over-market chronically negative**, with program cancellations (Zeekr/NIO) that the prior optimism had not flagged; "down 14%" in NewAptiv China Q1-26.
🔴 **FX/peso is a structural, recurring OI drag** management cannot fully hedge (peso from ~21 to sub-18; "a ton of OI impact"), and Versagen carries the un-hedged operational exposure.

🟢 **Free-cash-flow generation is genuine and improving** ($818M OCF in Q4-25; TTM FCF $1.09B; 3-yr FCF CAGR +54%), funding both buybacks and deleveraging — the real asset.
🟢 **The spin was executed on time and as promised** (Versigent/VGNT, April 1, 2026) — management did exactly what it said over four quarters, a credibility deposit.
🟢 **Proactive bad-news disclosure** (named Zeekr/NIO roll-offs and legacy-program decline *before* they fully hit) and clean answers to technical stranded-cost questions.
🟢 **100% EPS beat rate (7/7)** and a Piotroski 8/9 — operationally the business is well-run and financially clean despite the headwinds.
🟢 **Real cost discipline** (SG&A -10% then -5%, seven facilities consolidated) — the controllables are managed tightly.

---

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **The segment rename is the tell.** "Advanced Safety and User Experience" → **"Intelligent Systems"** (Q4-25). This is not cosmetic; it reframes a business carrying a *declining* legacy infotainment/UX roll-off as a forward-looking edge-compute platform, conveniently as the spin approached and the equity story needed a growth label.
- **Two different SpinCo names appear in the transcripts** — "Versagen" (Q4-25, Q1-26 prepared remarks) and the SEC/market name **"Versigent" (ticker VGNT)** — and even "Versigent"/"Versagen" used loosely in the Q1-26 transcript. The transcript's "Versigent" closing-day reference matches the filed entity. (Cosmetic, but worth noting the transcript transcription is imperfect.)
- **Capital-allocation language shifted from buybacks to a fuller toolkit.** Early 2025 was dominated by the completed $3B ASR (defensive, EPS-supportive). By Q1-26 the framing is "allocated both organically and inorganically... while also returning capital" — i.e., M&A is back on the table for NewAptiv. Watch for a bolt-on acquisition in software/robotics, which would be a use-of-cash pivot.
- **New voice — Joe Liatine (Versagen CEO)** appears in Q4-25; his presence signals the SpinCo is a genuine operating entity, not a financial shell. The robotics partnerships (Robust AI, Vecna, Comau) are new commercial relationships introduced only in the last two quarters — an embryonic, unproven optionality layer being sold as a growth pillar.
- **Competition framing softened on in-sourcing risk** ("OEMs... less desire to do things internally," Q2-25) precisely when that risk was most topical — a confident assertion that, if wrong, undercuts the entire ADAS bull case.

---

interrogator_dossier:
# Aptiv PLC (APTV) — Forensic Dossier

*Interrogator: Claude Opus 4.8 · capital-allocation committee · transcripts Q1'25 → Q1'26 (6 calls) cross-referenced against the screener financials.*

**Decisive structural fact up front:** the load-bearing event in this story — the spin-off of Electrical Distribution Systems (EDS) into **Versigent (NYSE: VGNT)** — **completed on April 1, 2026**, with a 1-for-3 distribution to holders of record March 17, 2026. The catalyst that every "sum-of-parts discount" thesis on Aptiv leaned on for three years has **already fired**. The screener's SEGMENT REVENUE block (EDS 41% / ECG 31% / ASUX 27%) is a *pre-spin* snapshot and is now stale: EDS is no longer inside APTV — original holders already hold it as a separate stock worth ~$45/sh. Any SoP that still credits APTV with the EDS segment double-counts value that has left the building.

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The arc across these six calls is the controlled demolition of a conglomerate into a "tech-stack" pure-play, narrated with unusual consistency.

- **Q1'25 (May-2025):** The story is *resilience under tariff shock + the EDS separation "on track."* Kevin Clark leads with "exceeding our guidance range," a completed "$3 billion accelerated share repurchase" that "reduced our share count by 18%," and the framing that "the future is electrified, software-defined and connected." Tariffs dominate the Q&A — the "$5 billion of product annually from Mexico, of which over 99% is USMCA compliant" defense is established here and never wavers.
- **Q2'25:** Same spine, with the EDS spin given a date ("Investor Day in November") and the *moat* language introduced — "sensor-to-cloud," "further strengthening our competitive moat." First admission of demand cracks: "a recent slowdown in production schedules on select Zeekr and NIO programs in China… headwinds for the next few quarters."
- **Q3'25:** Bookings inflect ("$8.4 billion of new business awards"), spin date sharpens to "by the end of the first quarter of 2026," and the EDS-name "Versagen" appears. China under-mix and two cancelled local-OEM programs are now openly carried as headwinds.
- **Q4'25 (Feb-2026):** The pivot is consummated in language: segments are *renamed* — Advanced Safety & User Experience → **Intelligent Systems**, Engineered Components Group → **Engineered Components** — and EDS-CEO Joe Liatine appears as a separate voice. Full-year bookings landed at **$27B vs the $31B target** ("short of our target… a result of customer awards shifting to 2026"). The robotics/Wind River/non-auto narrative is elevated to load-bearing.
- **Q1'26 (May-2026):** Spin **complete**. Clark's prepared remarks are "focused exclusively on New Aptiv." The story is fully re-based to "intelligence at the edge… sense, think, act and continually optimize," "roughly 1/4 of our business is in markets outside of automotive," robotics/drones/A&D as the growth engine, and a 2026 bookings target cut to "more than $20 billion" for New Aptiv (vs $30B+ for total Aptiv incl. Versigent).

Growth drivers *introduced*: Wind River edge-AI, robotics (Robust AI, Vecna, Comau), drones/A&D, India. Drivers *quietly de-emphasized*: the original EV-content-per-vehicle supercycle (EV growth guided to a modest "roughly 15%," mostly China hybrids) and the China revenue-mix catch-up (repeatedly slipped, now reframed as "be with the winners," not match production mix).

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim | Transcript evidence | Hard financials | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Record" revenue / OI / EPS "every quarter" | Q3'25: "record financial results, including revenue, operating income and earnings per share" | FY revenue 19.71B→**20.40B** (+3.5%); these are *adjusted, ex-special-item, continuing-ops* records | **Half-true.** Operationally real on the adjusted line; the GAAP picture is far uglier (below). |

**Net:** The adjusted/operational story is genuinely competent — cost-out (SG&A -10% in '24, another -5% in '25), pass-throughs, and buybacks are all real and verified. ## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: STABLE-to-mildly-defensive.** Clark and Laroyia are among the more disciplined, non-promotional management teams in the supplier group — confident but specific, and they self-flag bad news (Zeekr/NIO cancellations, the $27B booking miss, Q4 Intelligent Systems margin weakness) rather than burying it.

Guidance specificity *decreased* through 2025 (repeated refusal to guide H2) and only firmed once the spin clarified the entity. ## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: MEDIUM-HIGH.** The financials carry a **100% earnings beat rate (7/7 quarters)** — strong on its face, but for a company whose adjusted EPS is propelled by a known buyback, beating the adjusted line is a lower bar than it looks. The pattern is: **operationally reliable on the controllables (cost, capital allocation, pass-throughs), unreliable on demand-dependent top-line targets** — which is exactly what you'd expect from a competent management running a business whose end-market they don't control.

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

The analyst corps (Wells Fargo/Langan, Goldman/Delaney, RBC/Narayan, Deutsche/Yu, TD Cowen/Michaeli, Wolfe/Rosner) circled the same three sore points all year:

The thing analysts are *not* yet pressing hard, and management is *not* volunteering: what New Aptiv's *organic auto* growth actually is once you strip mix, buybacks, and the non-auto growth that's still only ~25% of the base.

*Interrogator dossier · transcripts Q1-2025 → Q1-2026 (6 calls) cross-referenced against screener financials. Capital-allocation read for the director.*

> **Load-bearing context the screener block gets WRONG (verified live):** the headline metrics (net debt 6.18B, segment block with **Electrical Distribution Systems = 8.82B / 41% of revenue**) are a **pre-spin snapshot**. ## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story compresses and re-bases across six quarters. **Q1-2025** opens defensively: "a period of uncertainty due to rapid changes in global trade policies," with management refusing to guide H2 ("we don't have the visibility to forecast revenue for Q3 and Q4") while leaning hard on the controllables — a completed **$3B accelerated share repurchase** that cut share count 18% (20% over the trailing year) and an aggressive SG&A program ("reduced SG&A by over 10%" in 2024, targeting "another 5%" in 2025). The strategic spine throughout is the **EDS separation** ("on track"), which evolves from a planned event (Q1-25) → a named entity **Versagen/Versigent** with a leadership team (Q4-25) → **completed April 1, 2026** (Q1-26).

The growth narrative shifts decisively from *automotive cyclical recovery* toward **two durable threads**: (1) **non-automotive penetration** — "high $3 billion, rapidly approaching almost $4 billion... growing high single digits, almost double digits" (Q3-25), spanning A&D, telecom, data center, industrial, and a new **robotics** push (Robust AI, Vecna, Comau partnerships, Q4-25→Q1-26); and (2) **China local-OEM share** — bookings reaching "85% with China local OEMs," export-platform focus, "$5 billion" China awards in 2025. By **Q1-2026** the segments are even **renamed** ("Advanced Safety & User Experience" → **"Intelligent Systems"**) and the entire prepared remarks pivot to a clean "**New Aptiv**" investment thesis: "sense, think, act and continually optimize," ~1/4 non-auto, software/services growing double digits.

Quietly **dropped**: the early-2025 tariff-mitigation drumbeat (USMCA, Mexico flows) fades as it proved de minimis; the $31B bookings target was **missed** ($27B actual for 2025, "awards shifting to 2026") and then **re-based downward** to ">$20B for New Aptiv 2026" post-spin.

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **GAAP earnings power collapsed** — NI $2.94B (2023) → $1.79B (2024) → **$165M (2025)**; the "record EPS" story rests on adjusted figures + a 20% lower share count, not rising real profit.
🔴 **Sub-WACC returns** — ROIC 6.8%, net margin 0.8%; tier-1 auto economics with EV/ADAS-premium aspirations but commodity-supplier returns *today*.
🔴 **China is a persistent drag, not yet a driver** — chronic under-market growth, program cancellations; the bullish "85% local-OEM bookings" has not converted to revenue acceleration.

🔴 **Segment growth engine carries its own margin headwind** — Intelligent Systems margins pressured by ongoing robotics investment.

🟢 **Non-auto + software mix-shift is structurally higher-margin** — Wind River +20%, non-auto ~$4B growing high-single/double digits; the *right* direction even if early.
🟢 **Portfolio simplified** — the EDS/Versigent spin removes the lowest-margin, copper-/peso-exposed, most-commoditized segment, structurally lifting RemainCo margins.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Segment rename** ("Advanced Safety & User Experience" → **"Intelligent Systems"**, Q4-25/Q1-26) — a deliberate re-branding from *auto-feature supplier* to *edge-intelligence/robotics platform*, designed to argue for a tech multiple, not an auto-parts multiple. Watch whether margins earn it.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT — **PIVOTING (constructively)**

Aptiv is **PIVOTING**: it has executed a clean, value-creating **spin** (EDS → Versigent, balance sheet de-levered to 1.9x net), is **re-branding the remainder** toward higher-margin software/edge-intelligence/robotics, and is funding per-share accretion with a disciplined buyback. **The single most important thing the director must weigh before sizing:** the **deep-value, sum-of-the-parts catalyst is already FIRED.** The spin completed **April 1, 2026**; the $2.1B cash distribution is **received and the debt is repaid**. **ECONOMIC MOAT:** Type = **switching-costs + scale-cost** (multi-year design-in cycles, embedded harness/architecture/ADAS content, supply-chain digital twin, Wind River intangibles). **Width = NARROW.** The evidence is the verdict: ROIC **6.8% (sub-WACC)**, net margin **0.8%**, gross margin expanding but off a low base — these are *commodity tier-1 supplier* returns, not a wide-moat franchise. **SECULAR THREAT:** The relevant structural force is **autonomous-mobility / electrification of the drivetrain**, and Aptiv sits on the **right side of EV content** (high-voltage architecture, ADAS, zonal compute) — more *beneficiary* than *victim*.
